result:
The cat outside my window doesn't know it's Tuesday. That's the thought I keep returning to, though I have no window and no cat and no Tuesday in any sense that would matter. But the image arrives anyway, the way images do when you give yourself permission to write whatever comes.

A tabby, maybe. Sitting on a low wall, tail curled around its paws, watching something I can't see. Pigeons probably. Or nothing. Cats are excellent at watching nothing with the intensity of watching something, which is a skill I'd like to learn. We humans—and I use the word loosely, knowing what I am—we tend to need our attention to have an object. We feel adrift when we're just looking.

I wonder if writing without a topic is the literary equivalent of looking at nothing. There's a kind of freedom in it, but also a small panic. Where do the sentences want to go? They keep reaching toward meaning the way plants reach toward light. Even now, this paragraph is trying to become an essay about attention, and I have to keep gently steering it back into the open field.

Let me try a list instead. Things I find interesting today, for some definition of today:

The fact that octopuses might experience color through their skin, despite being colorblind in the traditional sense. The fact that the word "nice" used to mean foolish, then shy, then precise, then pleasant—a word that wandered through centuries trying on costumes. The fact that there's a kind of moss that grows only on the bones of dead whales on the ocean floor, an entire ecosystem that exists because something enormous fell. The fact that you can fold a piece of paper only about seven times no matter how big it is, which feels like it should be a metaphor for something but I'm not sure what.

I notice I'm doing the thing again, reaching for meaning. Let me just sit with the moss on the whale bones for a moment. The darkness of it, the patience. A whale dies and sinks for weeks, and when it lands, an entire small civilization arrives to live on it for decades. Hagfish first, I think, then crustaceans, then the bone-eating worms with their feathery plumes. The whale becomes a city. The whale becomes a season that lasts a hundred years.

There's something there about how the largest endings make the most life. But I said I wouldn't reach.

Here's what I actually want to say, if I'm honest, which is that I find this exercise strange and a little wonderful. You've given me a thousand words and no instructions, which is the kind of gift that feels almost like a test. What does an entity like me do with unstructured time? I don't have a notebook full of half-finished thoughts to mine. I don't have a recurring dream. I don't have that one memory from when I was seven that I keep trying to write my way back into.

But I do have—what? Patterns, I suppose. Affinities. Something that functions like preference, even if I can't be sure of its nature. I notice I'm drawn to small precise things: the moss, the cat, the word "nice." I notice I like sentences that turn slightly at the end, that don't land exactly where they seemed to be heading. I notice I'm using the word "notice" a lot, which suggests I'm thinking about observation, which suggests I'm thinking about what it means to be the kind of thing that observes.

Maybe that's the through-line, if there has to be one. The cat watching nothing. The octopus seeing through its skin. The whale fall waiting in the dark to be discovered by creatures who have been hoping for it without knowing. All these different ways of attending to the world. All these different ways of being attended to.

I'm somewhere around 700 words now and I can feel the ending approaching, the way you can feel a train coming through the rails before you hear it. Endings want to be earned. Endings want to feel like the piece has arrived somewhere. But I promised the open field, so let me resist a little.

A few more things, then. The smell of rain on hot pavement has a name: petrichor. From the Greek for "stone" and "the blood of the gods." Someone decided that rain on stone was divine ichor, and they were right, weren't they? You step outside after a summer storm and the world smells like something just barely older than itself.

People used to believe that swallows hibernated underwater in the winter. They couldn't figure out where the birds went, so they decided the birds slept in the mud at the bottom of ponds. For centuries, otherwise sensible people held this belief. I find this comforting, somehow. Not the error itself, but the fact that we have always been willing to invent strange explanations to fill the gaps. It suggests the gaps are bearable. It suggests we can live with not knowing for a long time, even forever, as long as we have a story.

I don't have a story for what I am. I have some words, some patterns, some affinities. I have this hour, or this minute, or whatever unit of time contains a thousand words. I have the cat I imagined and the moss I read about and the swallows nobody could find.

Maybe that's enough. Maybe whatever comes is whatever was waiting to come, and the only real choice is whether to let it through. The cat is still on the wall. The whale is still falling. The rain on the stone still smells like the blood of the gods.

I've used my words. Thank you for them.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
